reasoning.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, sheet kinetic inductance decreases with increasing film thickness, following an approximate inverse (1/d) power law, with thinner films exhibiting dramatically higher kinetic inductance due to dirty-limit transport behavior.
reasoning.pattern_observed: An exceptionally strong, monotonically decreasing relationship between film thickness and sheet kinetic inductance is observed across all 12 NbSe2 samples. At the thinnest extreme, Zaman_2025_D1 (1.99 nm) shows Lk,sq = 1195 pH/sq, while the thickest sample Zaman_2025_D12 (52.12 nm) shows only 4 pH/sq — a ~300-fold range. The relationship is clearly super-linear on a linear scale and consistent with an inverse power law (approximately 1/d). The transition from dirty-limit (D1, D2) through intermediate (D3–D7) to clean-limit (D8–D12) transport regimes tracks monotonically with both increasing thickness and decreasing kinetic inductance, reinforcing that the underlying physics is disorder-driven suppression of superfluid density in thinner films. No outliers or reversals are observed in the rank ordering.

supporting_records.reason: Thinnest sample at 1.99 nm with highest Lk,sq of 1195 pH/sq (1.195 nH/sq); classified as dirty_limit. Provides the extreme anchor point demonstrating that ultra-thin NbSe2 has dramatically enhanced kinetic inductance.
Thickest sample at 52.12 nm with lowest Lk,sq of 4 pH/sq; classified as clean_limit. Provides the opposite extreme anchor, completing the ~300x dynamic range that defines the correlation.
At 4.08 nm, Lk,sq = 282 pH/sq, still in dirty_limit regime. Strongly consistent with 1/d trend and confirms the relationship holds at the few-nm scale.
At 5.86 nm, Lk,sq = 191 pH/sq, intermediate transport regime. Continues smooth monotonic decrease, bridging dirty and intermediate regimes.
At 10.07 nm, Lk,sq = 56 pH/sq, clean_limit. Represents the crossover point where the film transitions to clean-limit behavior, consistent with authors' claim about regime-dependent behavior.

At 24.73 nm, Lk,sq = 8 pH/sq, clean_limit, state_of_the_art coherence tier. Shows that even in the clean limit, the 1/d trend persists, and this sample achieves practical kinetic inductance values relevant to quantum circuit design.
At 7.13 nm, Lk,sq = 143 pH/sq, intermediate regime. Provides a well-resolved data point in the mid-thickness range confirming no anomalous deviations from monotonic behavior.
At 7.98 nm, Lk,sq = 135 pH/sq; nearly the same thickness as D4 but slightly thicker with slightly lower Lk,sq — fine-grained consistency within the intermediate regime.
At 8.81 nm, Lk,sq = 124 pH/sq, intermediate. Continues the monotonic decrease with well-resolved step from D5.
At 8.9 nm, Lk,sq = 107 pH/sq. Very similar thickness to D6 (8.81 nm) but noticeably lower Lk,sq, suggesting non-trivial sensitivity to sub-nm thickness differences near the dirty-to-clean crossover.
At 11.89 nm, Lk,sq = 49 pH/sq, clean_limit. Consistent with trend and confirms smooth continuation from D8.
At 30.2 nm, Lk,sq = 5 pH/sq, clean_limit. Confirms the trend flattens at larger thicknesses as expected from a 1/d law approaching bulk behavior.

complicating_records.reason: D6 (8.81 nm, 124 pH/sq) and D7 (8.9 nm, 107 pH/sq) differ by only 0.09 nm in thickness but by ~16% in Lk,sq. This raises a question about whether thickness measurement precision at sub-nm levels is sufficient to fully explain variance, or whether local disorder fluctuations contribute additional scatter near the dirty-to-clean crossover.

alternative_explanations: Disorder and sheet resistance co-vary with thickness in NbSe2 exfoliated or transferred films: thinner flakes have higher normal-state sheet resistance (Rn,sq) and are in the dirty limit (mean free path << coherence length), which independently drives up kinetic inductance through the Drude-BCS relationship Lk,sq ~ hRn,sq / (pi * Delta). The 1/d trend may therefore be a secondary consequence of disorder scaling rather than a geometric 1/d effect per se.

Crystal quality and proximity effects may degrade differently with thickness. Ultra-thin NbSe2 (1–4 nm) is known to be sensitive to substrate-induced disorder, surface oxidation, and environmental contamination, all of which suppress superfluid density and elevate kinetic inductance independently of geometric thickness arguments.

The crossover from dirty-limit to clean-limit behavior (as labeled in transport_regime field) introduces a physical regime change that itself changes the scaling law. The relationship may not be a single power law but a piecewise function with different exponents in each regime, making the simple 1/d description an approximation valid only in certain thickness windows.

All 12 samples originate from a single study (Zaman_2025) with a single material system (NbSe2) on a single substrate (high-resistivity Si). The correlation may be specific to this material family and fabrication approach and may not generalize to other superconductors or deposition methods.

Quantum confinement effects on the order parameter in atomically thin NbSe2 could contribute to Tc suppression and gap reduction at the thinnest samples, which would independently reduce superfluid density and raise Lk,sq beyond what a simple 1/d geometric argument would predict.
missing_evidence: Data from other superconducting material systems (e.g., NbN, TiN, MoRe) measured in the same thickness range to test whether the 1/d relationship is universal or NbSe2-specific.

Explicit sheet resistance (Rn,sq) measurements at each thickness to test whether Lk,sq correlates more tightly with Rn,sq than with d directly, which would indicate the disorder/dirty-limit explanation dominates over pure geometric scaling.

Samples with thicknesses between 10–25 nm (D9 at 11.89 nm and D10 at 24.73 nm have a significant gap) to better characterize the crossover regime and determine whether the power law exponent changes with thickness.
Kinetic inductance measurements on the same NbSe2 thicknesses but on different substrates to assess substrate-induced disorder contributions.

Temperature-dependent superfluid density measurements (e.g., from resonator frequency vs. T) to independently verify the dirty-vs-clean limit assignments and quantify the London penetration depth at each thickness.
Multiple independent samples at the same nominal thickness to assess fabrication reproducibility and confirm that the scatter (e.g., D6 vs D7) reflects physics rather than measurement uncertainty.
Measurements from groups other than Zaman_2025 to enable cross-laboratory validation of the reported Lk,sq values and the proposed 1/d scaling.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.72 (high end of the 0.70–0.75 band) based on the following considerations. Strengths: (1) All 12 samples exhibit a perfectly monotonic rank ordering of Lk,sq with thickness — no reversals whatsoever; (2) The dynamic range is enormous (~300x in Lk,sq across ~26x in thickness), making statistical noise essentially irrelevant; (3) The transport regime labels (dirty → intermediate → clean) co-vary monotonically with thickness, providing physical mechanistic corroboration; (4) Author-stated correlation claims are directly supported by the numerical data in the corpus. Limitations preventing higher confidence: (1) All 12 samples come from a single study and a single material system (NbSe2), introducing strong single-source bias; (2) The causal mechanism is ambiguous — the 1/d trend may reflect disorder scaling rather than pure geometric effects; (3) No cross-material validation is available in the 154-sample corpus; (4) The minor inconsistency between D6 and D7 (nearly identical thickness but non-trivial Lk,sq difference) hints at sub-nm thickness uncertainty. The finding is well-supported within NbSe2 but should be stated as material-specific pending broader evidence.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: No derived field concern: kinetic_inductance_sheet_pH_sq is a directly measured microwave resonator parameter (extracted from resonant frequency shifts), not derived from Tc or other fields in this corpus. This distinguishes it from the BCS gap findings previously flagged as derived_field_artifacts.
reasoning.negative_result_note: Not applicable — a strong positive (directional) result is observed.

reasoning.hypothesis: There is a meaningful correlation between film thickness and superconducting critical temperature (Tc) in superconducting thin films used for quantum computing applications.
reasoning.pattern_observed: No coherent cross-material pattern is visible. Within individual materials, the picture is mixed: NbSe2 (Zaman_2025_D1–D5, D8) shows a clear positive correlation where Tc rises from 3.5 K at ~2 nm to 7.0 K at ~10 nm — a physically well-understood thickness-dependent suppression of superconductivity in the ultra-thin limit. NbN (Bøttcher_2025, Wu_2026) shows no clear thickness trend: Tc is 10–12 K across thicknesses of 5–50 nm, with variation more plausibly attributed to deposition method and annealing. Al (Hedrick_2026, all 9 samples) is entirely uninformative — all samples are 200 nm with Tc ranging only 1.05–1.41 K, with substrate rather than thickness driving variance. PtSi (Nanayakkara_2026, 6 samples) shows no variation at all: constant Tc = 0.9 K at 25 nm. Re (Wang_2026, 5 samples) is similarly invariant: Tc = 1.9 K at 150 nm across all devices. The seeding correlation from Chang_2025 concerns Au encapsulation thickness affecting the proximity-effect-suppressed gap in a bilayer geometry, which is a physically distinct mechanism from intrinsic film Tc vs. self-thickness. Pooled across materials, the data are dominated by single-thickness clusters that provide no cross-thickness leverage per material, and the materials span wildly different intrinsic Tc values (0.9–12 K), making cross-material pooling statistically meaningless for this hypothesis.

supporting_records.reason: Thinnest NbSe2 sample at 1.99 nm shows lowest Tc of 3.5 K in the series, consistent with thickness-dependent Tc suppression.
At 4.08 nm, Tc = 4.8 K — intermediate, consistent with monotonic positive Tc-thickness relationship within NbSe2.
At 5.86 nm, Tc = 5.5 K — continues the monotonic increase, supporting a within-material positive correlation for NbSe2.
At 7.13 nm, Tc = 6.0 K — further supports the monotonic NbSe2 trend.
At 7.98 nm, Tc = 7.0 K — near-saturation of the NbSe2 thickness dependence, consistent with approach to bulk Tc.
At 10.07 nm, Tc = 7.0 K — plateau reached, consistent with standard 2D superconductor thickness dependence approaching bulk value.

complicating_records.reason: All 9 Al samples are at identical thickness (200 nm), so no within-material thickness variation is present. Tc varies (1.05–1.41 K) due to substrate and surface treatment differences, not thickness. These samples contribute zero evidence on the hypothesis.
All 6 PtSi samples have identical thickness (25 nm) and identical Tc (0.9 K). No thickness variation is available; these samples neither support nor oppose the hypothesis.
Single thickness point (150 nm, Tc = 1.9 K) for Re; no thickness variation available to assess trend.

NbN at 5 nm shows Tc = 10.0 K, while Bøttcher_2025 NbN at 20 nm shows Tc = 12.0 K and Wu_2026_M2O at 50 nm shows Tc = 10.0 K. This non-monotonic apparent relationship across NbN samples is confounded by different deposition methods (ALD vs. ALD with ALE thinning) and different annealing conditions (1000°C anneal for Wu_2026_M2O), undermining any simple thickness-Tc claim within NbN.
NbN at 20 nm with Tc = 12 K, deposited by ALD on annealed HEMEX sapphire — the highest Tc in the dataset — does not fit any simple monotonic trend when compared to other NbN or non-NbN samples.

Ta film at 100 nm has Tc = 4.4 K; Potluri_2025_Table_I_Row_2 is Ta at 150 nm with Tc = 4.3 K; Bland_2025_Ta-on-Si at 200 nm has Tc = 4.2 K. This slight apparent decrease with increasing thickness in Ta is marginal (0.2 K over 100 nm) and could reflect substrate differences (Si vs. Al2O3), deposition chamber differences, or measurement variability rather than a true thickness effect.

Re (with Al/AlOx junction) at 150 nm has Tc = 1.9 K, far below Ta samples at similar or greater thickness. This is entirely explained by material identity (Re bulk Tc ~1.7 K vs. Ta ~4.5 K), not thickness, demonstrating that material composition is the dominant variable.

470 nm Ta-Hf films show Tc range of 6.00–6.21 K, essentially the same as 250 nm films (5.84–6.21 K), directly contradicting any hypothesis that greater thickness meaningfully changes Tc in this alloy system.

Ta (with Al/AlOx junction) at 200 nm has Tc = 4.2 K, identical to the pure Ta film at 200 nm (Bland_2025_Ta-on-Si), suggesting the junction addition and the specific deposition context do not alter Tc appreciably, and thickness effects are absent at this scale.

alternative_explanations: For NbN, deposition technique (ALD parameters, plasma chemistry), substrate (sapphire vs. silicon), and post-deposition annealing temperature dominate Tc variation, masking any thickness signal.
Substrate differences (Si vs. sapphire vs. Al2O3) could confound any thickness-Tc comparison across samples, since epitaxial strain and interface quality vary.

Ultra-thin film effects (below ~10 nm) where quantum confinement or disorder suppresses Tc are not captured in this dataset, which has a minimum thickness of ~100 nm for samples with Tc measured — so thickness-Tc physics at the relevant thin-film regime is simply absent from the evidence.

The seeding correlation from Chang_2025 describes Au encapsulation proximity effect on an adjacent superconductor's effective gap — a bilayer/heterostructure effect distinct from a film's intrinsic Tc dependence on its own thickness.
Confounding by substrate: Al on Si shows higher Tc (1.41 K) than Al on c-Al2O3 (1.05 K) at the same 200 nm thickness, demonstrating substrate-Tc coupling that is unrelated to thickness.

The Chang_2025 seeding correlation concerns proximity-effect suppression of the effective superconducting gap in a bilayer (Ta + Au encapsulation), which is a different physical mechanism (Cooper pair leakage into normal metal) from intrinsic Tc thickness dependence and does not apply to the single-layer films in this corpus.

Within NbSe2, the positive Tc-thickness correlation is a known 2D superconductor phenomenon (quantum size effects, enhanced fluctuations, reduced Cooper pair density at ultra-thin limit) rather than a generic thin-film effect applicable across materials.
Single-thickness clusters (all Al samples at 200 nm, all PtSi at 25 nm, all Re at 150 nm) structurally prevent within-material thickness analysis for the majority of samples in the dataset.
Deposition conditions (chamber pressure, target purity, sputtering power) co-vary with thickness in some studies and may be the true driver of any Tc differences observed.

Material identity is the dominant driver of Tc variation in this dataset, not film thickness. The large Tc differences (0.9–12 K) reflect intrinsic material differences (Al, PtSi, NbSe2, NbN, Re) rather than thickness effects.
Material identity is the primary determinant of Tc across this dataset; any apparent thickness-Tc covariation is epiphenomenal to material choice.
missing_evidence: Thickness series within a single material at fixed deposition conditions and substrate — e.g., Ta films at multiple thicknesses (10–300 nm) deposited identically, which would isolate thickness as a variable.
Al films at thicknesses below 50 nm where quantum size effects are expected to become relevant (Al coherence length ~1.6 µm; thin-film Tc suppression typically below ~5–10 nm).
NbN films spanning a wide thickness range (2–100 nm) deposited by the same ALD process on the same substrate to disentangle thickness from process variation.
Re films at multiple thicknesses, given that only a single thickness (150 nm) is represented across all Re samples.
Ta and Ta-Hf samples with both Tc and film_thickness_nm measured — Ta appears in prior findings but lacks thickness data in the current 30-sample subset.
Direct Tc measurements (not derived BCS gap) for the Chang_2025 Ta+Au encapsulation series to connect the seeding correlation to Tc rather than gap suppression.
Statistical controls for deposition method, substrate, and anneal temperature to partial out their contributions before assessing residual thickness effects.
Same-material, same-deposition-condition films systematically varied in thickness from ~5 nm to ~500 nm with Tc measured at each step — this would isolate the thickness variable.
Ultra-thin film samples (below 20 nm) of Ta, NbN, or Al where Tc suppression due to disorder or quantum size effects would be most pronounced.

NbN and Al samples with both Tc and thickness recorded — only 43 of 154 corpus samples have both fields, and the 13 available are not from NbN or Al, which are known to show strong thickness-Tc dependence.
Direct measurement of sheet resistance alongside Tc and thickness to separate disorder-driven Tc suppression from geometric thickness effects.
Thickness series from a single study on the same material/substrate system, rather than cross-study comparisons where many confounders are uncontrolled.

reasoning.derived_field_concern: Prior findings [derived_BCS_gap_meV_vs_film_thickness_nm__nbse2] and [derived_BCS_gap_meV_vs_film_thickness_nm__ta] were derived from Tc via the BCS formula (Δ = 1.764 k_B Tc), meaning they are mathematically identical to a Tc-vs-thickness relationship and carry no independent evidential weight. Any apparent correlation in those derived fields is a direct artifact of the Tc measurement and cannot be cited as corroborating evidence for a Tc–thickness connection.
reasoning.negative_result_note: The absence of a cross-material Tc–thickness pattern in this dataset is itself informative: it indicates that film thickness is not a universal or dominant predictor of Tc across the diverse superconducting materials used in quantum computing. Material identity, deposition method, substrate, and post-deposition treatment appear to be far stronger determinants of Tc than film thickness in the regimes studied here (most films 20–200 nm, well above ultra-thin limits). Engineering Tc in these systems likely requires controlling process parameters rather than simply adjusting film thickness.

reasoning.hypothesis: Higher deposition temperature is meaningfully associated with lower superconducting critical temperature (Tc) across superconducting thin films used in quantum computing applications.
reasoning.pattern_observed: The data show no consistent cross-material trend. Within Ta-Hf (83:17) on sapphire (Yang_2026, samples 21-28), there is a weak negative trend: Tc drops from ~6.17-6.21 K at 550°C to ~5.84-6.0 K at 850°C, spanning only ~0.37 K across a 300°C range — statistically suggestive but physically modest. Al samples (Hedrick_2026) show the opposite micro-trend: the cryogenically deposited sample (Row_3, -72°C) has Tc=1.15 K, slightly higher than the 200°C-deposited samples at Tc=1.05 K, and the RT-deposited Si-substrate samples (Rows 8-9, 25°C) have Tc=1.41 K — but substrate differences (c-Al2O3 vs Si) and transport regime differences confound this. Re films (Wang_2026, samples 12-17) are all deposited at 900°C with Tc=1.9 K, providing no within-material variation. Ta films (samples 18-20) span 22°C to 625°C with Tc ranging 4.2-4.4 K, showing no clear trend and involving different substrates (Si vs Al2O3). NbN (Bøttcher_2025, samples 10-11) is deposited at 400°C via ALD with Tc=12.0 K — a single deposition temperature providing no within-material variation. Cross-material comparison is dominated by material identity rather than deposition temperature: NbN at 400°C gives Tc=12 K while Re at 900°C gives Tc=1.9 K, confirming that intrinsic material properties swamp any deposition temperature effect when comparing across materials.

supporting_records.reason: Among the highest Tc values in the Ta-Hf series, deposited at the lowest temperature (550°C), consistent with a negative correlation between deposition temperature and Tc within this alloy.
Replicate at 550°C shows the highest Tc in the entire Ta-Hf dataset (6.21 K), reinforcing that lower deposition temperature corresponds to higher Tc within this material system.
Lowest Tc in the Ta-Hf series at the highest deposition temperature (850°C), consistent with the hypothesized negative correlation.
Second replicate at 850°C also shows suppressed Tc (6.0 K) relative to 550°C samples, supporting the within-material negative trend.

Cryogenic deposition yields slightly higher Tc (1.15 K) than room-temperature (200°C) Al films on the same substrate type (c-Al2O3, Tc=1.05 K), weakly consistent with a negative deposition temperature-Tc relationship within Al on sapphire.

However, the substrate is different (Si vs c-Al2O3), transport regimes differ (dirty vs clean limit), and the Tc difference is likely substrate- or disorder-driven rather than a deposition temperature effect.

complicating_records.reason: Ta at 22°C on Si gives Tc=4.4 K and Ta at 500°C on Al2O3 gives Tc=4.3 K — a tiny difference that is also confounded by different substrates, making it impossible to attribute any Tc change to deposition temperature alone.

Ta deposited at 625°C yields Tc=4.2 K — nearly identical to Ta deposited at much lower temperatures (Potluri samples), suggesting deposition temperature has negligible effect on Tc for Ta, in contrast to the hypothesis.

complicating_records.display_name: Wang_2026_Re film and Transmon samples (Re, 900°C, Tc=1.9 K)
complicating_records.reason: Re is deposited at the highest temperature in the dataset (900°C) yet yields a reasonable Tc=1.9 K. Cross-material comparison would erroneously suggest high deposition temperature suppresses Tc, but Re's Tc is intrinsically low regardless of deposition conditions. This exemplifies why cross-material comparison is misleading.

This sample at 650°C has Tc=6.2 K, slightly higher than the 550°C sample (6.17 K), which locally contradicts the negative trend within the Ta-Hf series and highlights the small magnitude of the effect relative to measurement scatter.

Second replicate at 650°C also shows Tc=6.18 K, marginally higher than some 550°C values (6.17 K), again indicating the within-material trend is very weak and non-monotonic at intermediate temperatures.

alternative_explanations: Material identity is the dominant driver of Tc, not deposition temperature — the ~10 K spread in Tc across materials (NbN at 12 K vs Al at 1.05 K) dwarfs any within-material variation attributable to deposition temperature.

Substrate effects confound the cross-material and even within-Al comparison: Al on Si (dirty limit, Tc=1.41 K) vs Al on c-Al2O3 (clean limit, Tc=1.05 K) likely reflects disorder and interface effects rather than deposition temperature.

For Ta-Hf films, the Tc variation with deposition temperature (≤0.37 K over 300°C) may reflect changes in crystalline phase, grain structure, or alloy homogeneity rather than a direct thermodynamic effect of deposition temperature on the superconducting condensate.

Deposition method differences (ALD for NbN, e-beam for Al, DC sputtering for Ta/Re/Ta-Hf) create non-comparable baselines; the same deposition temperature means something physically different across these processes.

Anneal temperatures (e.g., 1200°C post-deposition anneal for Re Transmon samples, Wang_2026) likely dominate over deposition temperature in determining final film microstructure and Tc, yet are not controlled for in a simple deposition-temperature analysis.
Film thickness, which is not uniformly reported, is a known confound for Tc in thin films and may covary with deposition temperature in ways not captured here.
missing_evidence: Systematic within-material deposition temperature series for NbN, Ta, and Re using identical substrates and deposition methods — currently only Ta-Hf has such a series.
Controlled experiments varying only deposition temperature while fixing substrate, film thickness, deposition rate, and post-processing for each material.

Structural characterization (XRD, phase identification) alongside Tc to distinguish whether deposition temperature effects act through phase selection, grain size, or residual stress rather than directly.

Ta deposition temperature series on a single substrate type (Si or sapphire) across a wide temperature range (e.g., 25°C to 700°C) to test whether the weak trend seen across Potluri and Bland samples is real.
Al deposition temperature series on a single substrate to decouple deposition temperature from substrate effects observed in Hedrick_2026.
Larger sample counts per deposition temperature point to assess whether the ~0.3 K variation in Ta-Hf Tc is statistically significant above measurement noise.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: The evidence is insufficient to support a general cross-material hypothesis. Only the Ta-Hf (83:17) series (Yang_2026, 8 samples across 4 temperatures) provides a controlled within-material test, and even there the Tc variation is modest (~0.37 K over 300°C) with non-monotonic behavior at intermediate temperatures (650°C points slightly exceeding 550°C in one replicate). The Al, Ta, Re, and NbN data are all heavily confounded by substrate differences, different deposition methods, different film thicknesses, and post-deposition processing. Cross-material comparison is dominated by intrinsic material properties. The prior approved finding for Ta-Hf specifically had confidence 0.72, which is well-supported, but the general cross-material hypothesis remains weak. The overall cross-material confidence is reduced to ~0.28, reflecting: (1) only one material system provides clean within-material evidence, (2) the effect size is small even where it exists, (3) multiple confounds prevent clean interpretation of other materials, and (4) the 28-sample dataset is spread across 6 materials and multiple deposition methods with insufficient replication per condition.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: No derived field dependency issue for Tc_K itself; Tc is a directly measured quantity. However, some prior findings in this run involve derived_BCS_gap_meV which is computed from Tc — those findings are artifacts of the derivation and do not independently support or refute the present hypothesis.
reasoning.negative_result_note: The hypothesis is not fully rejected — a weak within-material negative trend exists for Ta-Hf — but the general cross-material claim is not supported, which itself is informative: deposition temperature is not a universal predictor of Tc across different superconducting material families, and material identity dominates over processing temperature in determining Tc.

stated_correlations.notes: Negative correlation: higher RRR and longer l (clean limit) correlates with lower Tact and greater vortex-motion-induced microwave loss
stated_correlations.term_a: RRR / mean free path / superconducting limit

relevant_catchall.notes: Vortex activation temperature directly characterizes the vortex motion loss channel that dominates in clean-limit Ta films; tracking this across samples would allow systematic comparison of loss mechanisms and is the primary discriminating observable between type A and type B films in this study

Mean free path compared to coherence length determines clean vs dirty limit; l > xi (clean limit) means no vortex pinning and dominant vortex-motion loss; tracking l enables prediction of which loss mechanism will dominate in a given film

reasoning.hypothesis: In Ta thin films, longer mean free path (cleaner, less disordered films) is negatively correlated with vortex activation temperature, such that dirty-limit films with short mean free paths exhibit substantially higher vortex activation temperatures.
reasoning.pattern_observed: A strong negative correlation is visible across the 8 samples, all from the Bahrami_2026 series. The three dirty-limit samples (D6: l=20.4 nm, Tact=1.69 K; D7: l=12.9 nm, Tact=4.16 K; D8: l=13.3 nm, Tact=4.17 K) show dramatically elevated vortex activation temperatures compared to the five clean-limit samples (D1–D5: l=81.5–142.3 nm, Tact=0.29–0.57 K). The transition appears highly nonlinear: clean-limit samples cluster tightly at low Tact despite a ~60 nm spread in l, while the two dirtiest samples (D7, D8) reach Tact ~4.17 K — roughly 7–14x higher than the cleanest samples. Within the clean-limit group alone, there is no clear monotonic trend (e.g., D3 with l=81.5 nm has the lowest Tact=0.29 K, while D1 with l=109.3 nm and D5 with l=112.2 nm have higher Tact=0.54–0.57 K), suggesting the dominant effect is the clean/dirty regime boundary rather than a smooth continuous relationship across all values of l.

supporting_records.reason: Shortest mean free path (12.9 nm, dirty limit) paired with highest vortex activation temperature (4.16 K), representing the extreme dirty-limit anchor point most strongly supporting the negative correlation.

Nearly identical to D7 (l=13.3 nm, Tact=4.17 K), providing a near-replicate confirmation of the dirty-limit extreme, strengthening confidence that this is a real physical effect rather than a measurement outlier.

Intermediate dirty-limit sample (l=20.4 nm, Tact=1.69 K) falls between the dirty-limit extremes (D7/D8) and the clean-limit cluster, consistent with the negative trend and suggesting a continuous underlying mechanism.

reasoning.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, sheet kinetic inductance (Lk,sq) decreases monotonically with increasing film thickness, following an approximate inverse power-law relationship.
reasoning.pattern_observed: The data show an extremely strong negative monotonic relationship between film thickness and sheet kinetic inductance across all 12 NbSe2 samples. The relationship is consistent with an approximate 1/d power law: plotting log(Lk,sq) vs log(d) yields a near-linear trend. The transition from dirty-limit behavior (D1–D2) through intermediate (D3–D7) to clean-limit (D8–D12) transport regimes aligns with the thickness increase and the suppression of kinetic inductance, providing physical coherence to the trend. No sample deviates from the monotonic ordering.

supporting_records.reason: Thinnest sample (1.99 nm) exhibits the highest Lk,sq (1195 pH/sq) and is firmly in the dirty limit, anchoring the upper end of the 1/d relationship with the most extreme values.
Thickest sample (52.12 nm) exhibits the lowest Lk,sq (4 pH/sq) in the clean limit, anchoring the lower end and spanning the full dynamic range of the dataset.
At 4.08 nm, Lk,sq = 282 pH/sq, consistent with dirty-limit behavior and a large enhancement over the clean-limit value, supporting the 1/d trend at the thin end.
At 5.86 nm (intermediate regime), Lk,sq = 191 pH/sq, fitting cleanly between D2 and D4 in both thickness and inductance, consistent with continuous 1/d scaling.

At 10.07 nm, the first sample classified as clean-limit, Lk,sq drops sharply to 56 pH/sq, marking the regime transition and supporting the physical narrative that clean-limit samples have suppressed kinetic inductance relative to dirty-limit peers at similar thickness.
At 11.89 nm, Lk,sq = 49 pH/sq, continuing the monotonic decrease into the clean-limit regime without deviation.
At 24.73 nm (clean limit, state-of-the-art coherence tier), Lk,sq = 8 pH/sq, well below intermediate-regime values and consistent with the 1/d law at larger thickness.
At 30.2 nm, Lk,sq = 5 pH/sq, continuing the trend and showing that further thickness increase beyond ~25 nm yields diminishing returns in Lk,sq reduction.

complicating_records.reason: D7 (8.9 nm, 107 pH/sq) and D6 (8.81 nm, 124 pH/sq) are nearly identical in thickness (0.09 nm apart) yet differ by ~15% in Lk,sq. This suggests measurement variability or sample-to-sample disorder differences at similar nominal thicknesses, slightly complicating the precision of the 1/d claim at fine scales, though the overall monotonic ordering is preserved.

D8 (10.07 nm) shows a notable jump in the transport regime classification from 'intermediate' to 'clean_limit' relative to D7 (8.9 nm), with a larger-than-expected drop in Lk,sq (107 to 56 pH/sq over only ~1.2 nm). This may indicate that the regime transition is non-linear near this thickness, complicating a simple smooth 1/d power law in the 8–10 nm range.

alternative_explanations: The inverse thickness dependence of kinetic inductance is, in part, a fundamental geometric/electrodynamic effect: Lk,sq = mu_0 * lambda_L^2 / d, so even at fixed London penetration depth lambda_L, Lk,sq scales as 1/d. Part of the observed trend is thus expected from basic theory regardless of material-specific physics.

Thin NbSe2 films may have higher disorder (shorter mean free paths, surface scattering), which independently increases lambda_L (dirty-limit enhancement). The combined effect of geometric 1/d scaling and disorder-enhanced lambda_L could produce a steeper-than-1/d dependence at ultra-thin samples, explaining the very high Lk,sq at D1 (1.99 nm).

NbSe2 exhibits a charge-density-wave (CDW) phase that competes with superconductivity and becomes more prominent at lower thickness. CDW-induced suppression of superfluid density in ultra-thin samples could contribute to elevated kinetic inductance beyond simple thickness scaling.

Proximity to the superconductor-insulator transition (SIT) in the thinnest samples (D1, D2) could cause a divergence in kinetic inductance that is not purely a 1/d effect but rather a quantum phase transition phenomenon.

All 12 samples originate from a single study (Zaman_2025) and a single material system (NbSe2 on high-resistivity Si). The uniformity of substrate, deposition protocol, and measurement apparatus removes confounders but also means the finding is not yet independently replicated across labs or substrates.
missing_evidence: Independent replication of this thickness-Lk,sq trend in NbSe2 films by a different group or on a different substrate (e.g., sapphire, SiO2) to confirm the relationship is material-intrinsic rather than substrate- or process-specific.

Direct measurements of mean free path (l) and coherence length (xi) as a function of thickness to quantitatively test the dirty-limit vs. clean-limit regime assignments and their role in the Lk,sq enhancement.
Lk,sq data at intermediate thicknesses (12–24 nm) to determine whether the transition from the sharp drop in the 10–12 nm range to the near-plateau at 25–52 nm is gradual or abrupt.

Microwave resonator quality factor (Qi) data co-measured with Lk,sq for all 12 samples, to determine whether the high-Lk,sq samples also show degraded or improved coherence performance for quantum circuit applications.
Temperature-dependent Lk,sq measurements to confirm the BCS-like superfluid density depletion and its thickness dependence.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: Confidence is high (0.75) because: (1) all 12 samples show a perfectly monotonic negative relationship with no exceptions; (2) the dynamic range spans nearly three orders of magnitude in Lk,sq, making the trend unmistakable and not attributable to measurement noise; (3) the trend is physically well-motivated by both geometric (1/d) and disorder (dirty-limit lambda_L enhancement) arguments that are independently supported by the transport regime annotations; (4) author-stated claims explicitly document the 1/d dependence and the dirty-to-clean crossover; and (5) the ratio Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean rising from 0.7 to 15 with decreasing thickness adds an internally consistent control. The cap at 0.75 rather than higher reflects: single-study provenance (all samples from Zaman_2025, one material system, one substrate), the absence of independent replication, and the inability to fully disentangle geometric 1/d scaling from disorder-driven lambda_L changes without additional measurements.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: kinetic_inductance_sheet_pH_sq is a directly measured experimental observable (not a derived field in this dataset), so there is no derived-field artifact concern analogous to the BCS-gap findings flagged in prior runs. However, Lk,sq is inherently coupled to film thickness through the geometric factor (Lk,sq ~ lambda_L^2/d), meaning a pure 1/d dependence would exist even if lambda_L were constant. This geometric coupling means the correlation is partially definitional rather than entirely empirical, and should be interpreted accordingly.

relevant_catchall.notes: Vortex activation temperature directly characterizes the vortex motion loss channel that dominates in clean-limit Ta films; tracking this across samples would allow systematic comparison of loss mechanisms and is the primary discriminating observable between type A and type B films in this study

Mean free path compared to coherence length determines clean vs dirty limit; l > xi (clean limit) means no vortex pinning and dominant vortex-motion loss; tracking l enables prediction of which loss mechanism will dominate in a given film

reasoning.hypothesis: In Ta thin films, longer mean free path (cleaner, higher-RRR films) is negatively correlated with vortex activation temperature, such that dirty-limit films exhibit substantially higher vortex activation temperatures than clean-limit films.
reasoning.pattern_observed: A strong and visually clear negative correlation is present across the 8 samples. Dirty-limit samples (Bahrami_2026_D7: l=12.9 nm, Tact=4.16 K; Bahrami_2026_D8: l=13.3 nm, Tact=4.17 K; Bahrami_2026_D6: l=20.4 nm, Tact=1.69 K) show markedly elevated vortex activation temperatures compared to all five clean-limit samples (D1–D5), which cluster with l=81.5–142.3 nm and Tact=0.29–0.57 K. The transition between the two regimes is sharp and corresponds cleanly to the dirty/clean-limit boundary. Within the clean-limit cluster, the correlation is weak and noisy (e.g., D3 has l=81.5 nm and the lowest Tact=0.29 K, while D5 has l=112.2 nm and Tact=0.57 K, not monotonically ordered), but the between-regime separation is very large (~one order of magnitude in Tact).

supporting_records.reason: Shortest mean free path (12.9 nm, dirty limit) paired with the highest vortex activation temperature (4.16 K), the most extreme data point supporting the negative correlation.
Nearly identical to D7 (l=13.3 nm, Tact=4.17 K), providing strong internal replication of the dirty-limit extreme and confirming the result is not a single-sample artifact.
Intermediate dirty-limit sample (l=20.4 nm, Tact=1.69 K) sits between the dirty-limit cluster and clean-limit cluster, consistent with a monotonic trend across the regime boundary.
Longest mean free path in the dataset (142.3 nm, clean limit) paired with Tact=0.37 K, one of the lowest activation temperatures, consistent with the hypothesis direction.

Shortest mean free path among the clean-limit samples (81.5 nm) and the lowest activation temperature in that group (0.29 K); does not contradict the overall regime-level pattern, though it slightly inverts within-cluster ordering.

complicating_records.reason: Has the shortest mean free path among the clean-limit group (81.5 nm) yet the lowest Tact (0.29 K) in that group, inverting the expected within-cluster ordering and suggesting the correlation is not fine-grained within the clean limit.

l=109.3 nm, Tact=0.54 K — similar l to D5 (112.2 nm, 0.57 K) but also similar to D4 (125.2 nm, 0.33 K), indicating that within the clean-limit regime, Tact variation (0.29–0.57 K) is not well explained by mean free path differences alone.

l=112.2 nm yet Tact=0.57 K, the highest among the clean-limit samples, while D4 (l=125.2 nm) has lower Tact=0.33 K. This non-monotonic relationship within the clean-limit cluster indicates other factors modulate Tact in that regime.

alternative_explanations: The true driver may be the transport regime (dirty vs. clean limit) as a categorical variable rather than mean free path as a continuous predictor. The large between-regime gap in Tact could reflect a qualitative change in vortex pinning physics (e.g., collective vs. single-vortex creep) rather than a smooth linear dependence on l.

Disorder and defect density, which reduce mean free path, may also increase pinning center density, raising the energy barrier for vortex motion and thus Tact. In this view, l is a proxy for microstructural disorder rather than the direct physical cause of Tact differences.

Film microstructure (e.g., grain size, columnar vs. amorphous growth) could co-vary with mean free path and independently control vortex pinning landscapes, making l a correlated but non-causal variable.

All 8 samples are from a single study (Bahrami_2026) using the same deposition method and substrate, so the pattern could reflect a specific deposition-condition parameter (e.g., sputtering power or gas pressure) that simultaneously affects both l and Tact without a direct causal link between the two.

Within the clean-limit cluster, the scatter in Tact at similar l values (e.g., D1 vs. D4 vs. D5) suggests that additional variables such as film thickness, interface quality, or residual stress may modulate vortex activation temperature independently of mean free path.
missing_evidence: Samples from other materials systems (e.g., Nb, NbN, Al) to test whether the l–Tact relationship generalizes beyond Ta.
Intermediate mean free path samples (l ~ 30–80 nm) to map the transition between the dirty- and clean-limit behaviors more continuously.
Direct pinning landscape measurements (e.g., scanning Hall probe or magneto-optical imaging) to distinguish intrinsic vs. extrinsic vortex pinning contributions.
Upper critical field (Hc2) and lower critical field (Hc1) data to contextualize how l-dependent London penetration depth and coherence length changes affect vortex energetics.
Independent replication from a second research group or deposition system to confirm the trend is not instrument- or process-specific.
Film thickness information for all samples (only implicitly constrained) to rule out thickness as a confounding variable given the known Tc–thickness relationship in Ta.
Activation energy (U0) extracted from full thermally-activated flux-flow fits to disambiguate whether Tact differences reflect changes in pinning energy scale or attempt frequency.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: The between-regime separation is exceptionally clear and large: dirty-limit samples (D6, D7, D8) have Tact 3–14x higher than any clean-limit sample, and the pattern is consistent with the author-stated claim. Two near-identical dirty-limit replicates (D7, D8) provide internal validation. However, within the clean-limit cluster the correlation is weak and non-monotonic, indicating l alone does not continuously predict Tact at fine resolution. All 8 samples come from a single study, single material (Ta), and single deposition method, severely limiting generalizability. The sample count (n=8) is small. These limitations prevent a higher confidence rating, but the regime-level signal is robust enough to reach the 0.70 threshold with specific citations and clearly identified complicating cases.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: Mean free path is typically a derived quantity computed from resistivity and RRR measurements rather than directly measured; systematic errors in resistivity modeling (e.g., assuming bulk scattering parameters for thin films) could introduce correlated errors in l estimates. Vortex activation temperature extracted from thermally-activated flux-flow fits also involves model-dependent analysis. Both fields carry model-dependency, but since the raw data (resistivity, flux-flow voltage) are presumably directly measured and the trends are large in magnitude, this concern is moderate rather than disqualifying.

reasoning.hypothesis: In Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films, film thickness is meaningfully correlated with superconducting critical temperature (Tc), such that thicker films exhibit a systematically different Tc.
reasoning.pattern_observed: The 8 samples span only two thickness values: 250 nm (Rows 1-4) and 470 nm (Rows 5-8), all from Yang_2026 and all deposited identically. The 250 nm group has Tc values of 6.17, 6.20, 6.02, and 5.84 K (mean ~6.06 K). The 470 nm group has Tc values of 6.21, 6.18, 6.06, and 6.00 K (mean ~6.11 K). The means differ by only ~0.05 K, and the within-group spread (~0.36 K for 250 nm, ~0.21 K for 470 nm) is comparable to or larger than the between-group difference. There is no statistically meaningful or directionally consistent pattern: the highest individual Tc (6.21 K) belongs to a 470 nm sample (Row 5), but the second highest (6.20 K) is a 250 nm sample (Row 2). No monotonic trend is apparent.

supporting_records.reason: The highest Tc in the dataset (6.21 K) is from a 470 nm film, which could weakly suggest thicker films have marginally higher Tc, but this is offset by within-group variance.
A 250 nm film with Tc 6.17 K is close to the 470 nm mean, providing no clear thickness-dependent signal but contributing to the thin evidence base.

complicating_records.reason: This 250 nm film has the lowest Tc in the entire dataset (5.84 K), pulling the 250 nm group mean down, but this likely reflects within-group variability (e.g., deposition conditions, stoichiometry) rather than a thickness effect.
Tc of 6.20 K at 250 nm is nearly identical to Row 5 at 470 nm (6.21 K), directly undermining any directional thickness-Tc correlation.
Tc of 6.00 K at 470 nm overlaps fully with the lower end of the 250 nm group, showing no thickness-driven floor or ceiling effect.

alternative_explanations: Within-group Tc variance at fixed thickness (~0.36 K range at 250 nm) likely reflects other deposition variables (e.g., target condition, minor substrate variation, stoichiometric fluctuation) that dominate over any thickness effect in this narrow range.

Both thickness values (250 nm and 470 nm) may be well above any thickness-dependent Tc suppression threshold for Ta-Hf alloy films, placing all samples in a 'bulk-like' regime where thickness is irrelevant.

The literature seed (Chang_2025) describes proximity-effect suppression of the gap via Au encapsulation — a fundamentally different physical mechanism (proximity effect between dissimilar metals) than intrinsic thickness dependence of a single-material film, making the analogy to Ta-Hf indirect at best.

Differences between the two thickness groups may reflect batch-to-batch deposition variability rather than a systematic thickness effect, especially since all samples are from a single source (Yang_2026) with no independent replication.
missing_evidence: Tc measurements at additional thickness values (e.g., 10–100 nm, and >500 nm) to probe whether a thickness-dependent trend emerges at thinner or thicker regimes.
Samples from a second independent source or deposition run at the same thicknesses to distinguish batch variability from a genuine thickness effect.
Direct energy gap measurements (e.g., tunneling spectroscopy) at these two thicknesses to test whether the gap varies even if Tc does not.
Ta-Hf samples at the ultra-thin limit (<50 nm) where proximity and disorder effects are known to suppress superconductivity in related materials.
Controlled deposition series where only thickness is varied and all other parameters (power, pressure, temperature, target) are fixed, with sufficient replicates for statistical testing.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: Only two distinct thickness values are represented, both from a single study (Yang_2026), with 4 replicates each. The between-group mean difference (~0.05 K) is smaller than the within-group spread at 250 nm (~0.36 K), providing no statistical basis for a thickness-Tc correlation. The seeding literature claim (Chang_2025) involves a physically distinct proximity-effect mechanism in a different material system (Ta+Au bilayers) and does not directly support an intrinsic thickness-Tc relationship in Ta-Hf alloy films. There are no complicating confounders (all same material, substrate, deposition method), but the data simply lack the dynamic range and sample diversity to detect or exclude a trend. Confidence is set at 0.1 — no meaningful pattern is observed, and the data are insufficient to establish one.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: Not directly applicable here since Tc_K is a directly measured quantity. However, the prior approved finding [derived_BCS_gap_meV_vs_film_thickness_nm__ta] showing a negative gap-thickness correlation in Ta is a derived field artifact (gap computed from Tc via BCS), meaning any apparent gap-thickness trend in that prior finding is entirely inherited from a Tc-thickness trend — which the present direct Tc data do not support for Ta-Hf films in this thickness range.
reasoning.negative_result_note: The absence of a detectable Tc-thickness pattern in the 250–470 nm range for Ta-Hf (83:17) films is itself informative: it suggests that once Ta-Hf films exceed some lower thickness threshold (likely well below 250 nm), Tc reaches a plateau and further thickness variation in this range does not drive meaningful changes. This is physically plausible given the dirty-limit transport regime of all samples, where coherence length is short and bulk-like behavior likely sets in at modest thicknesses. This null result should discourage over-interpretation of the prior derived gap-thickness finding for Ta as indicative of a strong intrinsic thickness effect in Ta-family alloy films at technologically relevant thicknesses.

reasoning.hypothesis: For Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films deposited by DC magnetron sputtering on sapphire, increasing deposition temperature is negatively correlated with superconducting transition temperature (Tc).
reasoning.pattern_observed: A consistent negative trend is visible across both deposition series. In the first series (Rows 1-4): Tc decreases from 6.17 K at 550°C → 6.20 K at 650°C → 6.02 K at 750°C → 5.84 K at 850°C. In the second series (Rows 5-8): Tc decreases from 6.21 K at 550°C → 6.18 K at 650°C → 6.06 K at 750°C → 6.00 K at 850°C. Both series show a notable drop from 750°C to 850°C and a slight anomaly at 650°C (marginally higher than 550°C in both series), but the overall trend is clearly negative with the highest Tc values at the lowest deposition temperatures and the lowest Tc values at the highest deposition temperatures. The total Tc suppression across the 300°C range is ~0.33 K in series 1 and ~0.21 K in series 2 — modest but consistent.

supporting_records.reason: Lowest Tc (5.84 K) at highest deposition temperature (850°C) in series 1, representing the most extreme negative departure and anchoring the trend.
Lowest Tc (6.00 K) at highest deposition temperature (850°C) in series 2, independently replicating the suppression at 850°C.
Tc drops to 6.02 K at 750°C in series 1, continuing the monotonic decline from 550°C through 850°C.
Tc = 6.06 K at 750°C in series 2, replicating the intermediate suppression step seen in series 1.
Tc = 6.17 K at the lowest deposition temperature (550°C) in series 1, consistent with higher Tc at lower deposition temperature.
Highest Tc overall (6.21 K) at 550°C in series 2, reinforcing that low deposition temperature preserves Tc.

complicating_records.reason: Tc at 650°C (6.20 K) is marginally higher than at 550°C (6.17 K) in series 1, introducing a non-monotonic step at the low-temperature end. This slightly complicates a strict monotonic interpretation, though the value is within likely measurement uncertainty.

Similarly in series 2, Tc at 650°C (6.18 K) is marginally lower than at 550°C (6.21 K) but very close, and the 650°C value is still higher than 750°C and 850°C — so the non-monotonicity is minor but present at the low end.

alternative_explanations: Higher deposition temperature may alter the crystallographic phase or texture of Ta-Hf alloy films (e.g., promoting α-Ta over β-Ta-like phases or changing grain structure), which independently suppresses Tc regardless of a direct temperature-Tc relationship.
Increased deposition temperature could promote interdiffusion between the film and sapphire substrate, introducing interface defects or oxygen contamination that suppress superconductivity.

Thermal stress and differential thermal expansion between film and substrate at higher deposition temperatures could introduce strain states that modify the electronic density of states at the Fermi level, reducing Tc.

The slight Tc increase from 550°C to 650°C in both series could indicate an optimal crystallization temperature where film quality peaks, suggesting a non-monotonic underlying mechanism rather than a purely linear thermal suppression.
The effect may be confounded by deposition rate or argon pressure changes that accompany temperature changes in the sputtering process, rather than temperature per se.
missing_evidence: Structural characterization (XRD, TEM) at each deposition temperature to determine whether phase, texture, or grain size changes correlate with the Tc trend.
Data from other material systems (e.g., pure Ta, TaN, Nb) deposited under similar conditions to assess whether the negative Tc-deposition temperature trend is general or specific to Ta-Hf alloys.
Deposition temperatures below 550°C to determine if Tc continues to rise or plateaus, which would reveal whether 550°C is already near-optimal.
Film composition verification (RBS or EDX) at each temperature to rule out Hf segregation or evaporation as a confound.
Residual stress measurements to test the thermal strain hypothesis.

Identification of what differentiates the two deposition series (e.g., different deposition duration — the hypothesis seed mentions '1 ks films' suggesting anneal time or deposition time may vary) to understand why absolute Tc values differ slightly between series.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.72, matching the prior approved finding, because: (1) both independent series in Yang_2026_Table_2 show consistent negative trends from 750°C to 850°C; (2) the lowest Tc values appear at the highest deposition temperatures in both series without exception at the high-temperature end; (3) there are 8 samples covering 4 temperature points in duplicate, providing reasonable internal replication within a single material/substrate/method system; (4) the slight non-monotonicity at 550°C–650°C (where Tc is marginally higher at 650°C in series 1) introduces mild uncertainty about strict monotonicity, preventing confidence above 0.75; (5) all 8 samples are from a single study, single material, and single deposition method, limiting generalizability; (6) the effect size is small (~0.2–0.3 K over 300°C), which while consistent is within the range where systematic measurement artifacts could contribute. The prior approved finding at 0.72 is well-supported by the current data.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: No derived field dependency concern. Tc_K is a directly measured physical property, not derived from another measured field in this dataset.
reasoning.negative_result_note: Not applicable — a positive (in the sense of detecting a relationship) result is observed.

reasoning.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, higher sheet resistance (Ohm/sq) is associated with lower internal quality factor (Qi_internal), suggesting that increased disorder or proximity to the superconductor-insulator transition degrades microwave loss.
reasoning.pattern_observed: The data show no clear monotonic relationship between sheet resistance and Qi_internal across the six NbSe2 samples. The highest Qi (60,000 in Zaman_2025_D2 at 60 Ohm/sq) falls in the middle of the sheet resistance range, not at the low end. The lowest sheet resistance samples (D8: 10 Ohm/sq, Qi=7,000; D5: 19 Ohm/sq, Qi=12,000; D4: 25 Ohm/sq, Qi=24,000) show broadly increasing Qi with increasing sheet resistance, but D3 (29 Ohm/sq, Qi=7,000) breaks this trend. The highest sheet resistance sample (D1: 240 Ohm/sq, Qi=8,000) does show low Qi consistent with disorder-driven loss, but D2 (60 Ohm/sq) achieves the best Qi in the entire set, which contradicts a simple monotonic negative correlation. The overall picture is non-monotonic and dominated by scatter, with a weak suggestion that extreme sheet resistance (>200 Ohm/sq) may be harmful, but no robust trend across the intermediate range.

supporting_records.reason: Highest sheet resistance (240 Ohm/sq) in dirty-limit regime and lowest Qi (8,000), consistent with disorder-driven microwave loss degradation.
Intermediate sheet resistance (25 Ohm/sq) paired with moderately high Qi (24,000), loosely consistent with a trend where lower sheet resistance correlates with better Qi relative to D1.

complicating_records.reason: Best Qi in the dataset (60,000) at an intermediate sheet resistance (60 Ohm/sq), directly contradicting a simple monotonic negative correlation—this sample performs better than all lower sheet resistance samples.
Sheet resistance of 29 Ohm/sq (intermediate) but Qi of only 7,000, the lowest alongside D8, which breaks any local trend that lower sheet resistance implies higher Qi.

Lowest sheet resistance (10 Ohm/sq, clean-limit regime) but Qi of only 7,000, the worst among the lower sheet resistance samples. If the hypothesis were correct, this sample should show among the highest Qi.
Sheet resistance of 19 Ohm/sq with Qi of 12,000—lower than D4 (25 Ohm/sq, Qi=24,000), suggesting Qi does not track inversely with sheet resistance in this range.

alternative_explanations: Transport regime (dirty_limit vs. intermediate vs. clean_limit) may be a more relevant predictor than sheet resistance per se; D8 in the clean limit achieves low Qi despite low sheet resistance, suggesting regime-specific loss mechanisms.

Film thickness variations among the Zaman_2025 samples likely co-vary with sheet resistance and may independently control Qi via dielectric loss at interfaces or vortex dynamics, confounding any direct sheet resistance–Qi relationship.

Fabrication variability and unmeasured structural quality (grain size, surface roughness, stoichiometry) across these NbSe2 samples may dominate Qi differences, swamping any intrinsic sheet resistance dependence.

The Bøttcher_2025 NbN finding—that Qi is independent of sheet resistance (RN) near the superconductor-insulator transition—represents a distinct material system and deposition method (ALE) where quasiparticle losses are well controlled; NbSe2 vdW films may behave differently due to layer-dependent order parameter suppression.
Non-equilibrium quasiparticle populations or substrate coupling (high-resistivity Si) may vary across samples due to differences in film-substrate interface quality rather than bulk sheet resistance.
missing_evidence: Larger sample set (>20 samples) spanning a continuous range of sheet resistance values in systematically controlled NbSe2 films with fixed thickness and deposition conditions.
Simultaneous measurement of film thickness for each sample to decouple sheet resistance from thickness-dependent superconductivity suppression.
Two-level system (TLS) loss tangent measurements to determine whether Qi differences are dominated by dielectric loss, quasiparticle loss, or vortex loss.
Data from other NbSe2 substrates or deposition methods to assess whether the Si (high-resistivity) substrate is a confound.
Comparable data from NbN or other materials where sheet resistance is systematically varied to contextualize the Bøttcher_2025 null result against this NbSe2 dataset.
Power-dependence of Qi to discriminate TLS-dominated from quasiparticle-dominated loss regimes across samples.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: Only 6 samples are available, all from a single study (Zaman_2025) on NbSe2, and the data show no consistent monotonic relationship. The best-performing sample (D2, Qi=60,000) sits at intermediate sheet resistance, while the clean-limit sample with the lowest sheet resistance (D8) performs among the worst. This directly undermines the hypothesis. The only weak support is that the extreme high-sheet-resistance outlier (D1, 240 Ohm/sq) does show low Qi. The prior approved finding for this hypothesis is already rated confidence 0.2 (inconclusive), and the new evidence does not improve this—if anything, closer inspection of D8 and D3 further weakens the case. The Bøttcher_2025 null result in NbN provides an independent reason to expect no simple correlation. Confidence remains at 0.2 given the non-monotonic scatter, small N, and absence of controlled covariates.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: No derived field dependency issue; both Qi_internal and sheet_resistance_Ohm_sq are directly measured quantities in this dataset.
reasoning.negative_result_note: The absence of a clear monotonic pattern between sheet resistance and Qi_internal in these NbSe2 samples is itself informative: it suggests that sheet resistance alone is not a reliable predictor of microwave loss in NbSe2 thin films, and that other factors (transport regime, film thickness, interface quality, structural disorder) likely play a dominant role. This is broadly consistent with the Bøttcher_2025 null result in NbN, suggesting that proximity to the superconductor-insulator transition does not deterministically degrade Qi across different superconducting material systems.

reasoning.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, increasing film thickness is positively associated with higher superconducting critical temperature (Tc), reflecting thickness-dependent suppression of superconductivity in the ultra-thin limit.
reasoning.pattern_observed: A clear, monotonically positive relationship between film_thickness_nm and Tc_K is visible across all 6 NbSe2 samples from Zaman_2025. Tc rises from 3.5 K at 1.99 nm (D1) through 4.8 K at 4.08 nm (D2), 5.5 K at 5.86 nm (D3), 6.0 K at 7.13 nm (D4), and plateaus near 7.0 K for both D5 (7.98 nm) and D8 (10.07 nm). The trend is strongly monotonic up to ~8 nm, after which Tc appears to saturate near the bulk NbSe2 value of ~7.2 K. The direction is unambiguously positive and consistent with quantum-size-effect / disorder-driven suppression of superconductivity in the ultra-thin regime.

supporting_records.reason: The thinnest sample at 1.99 nm shows the lowest Tc of 3.5 K, anchoring the low end of the trend and demonstrating severe Tc suppression at the ultra-thin limit. Its dirty_limit transport regime is consistent with strong disorder-driven pair-breaking.
At 4.08 nm, Tc rises to 4.8 K, a substantial jump from D1. This supports a steep thickness-dependence in the 2–4 nm range and is consistent with the transition out of the most disordered regime.
Tc of 5.5 K at 5.86 nm continues the monotonic increase; the shift to 'intermediate' transport regime aligns with growing film coherence as thickness increases.
At 7.13 nm and Tc = 6.0 K, the trend continues but begins to flatten, suggesting approach toward bulk behavior.
Tc reaches 7.0 K at 7.98 nm, consistent with near-bulk NbSe2 behavior; the intermediate transport regime transitioning toward clean limit supports restored bulk-like pairing.

complicating_records.reason: At 10.07 nm, Tc is also 7.0 K — identical to D5 at 7.98 nm despite being ~25% thicker. This suggests Tc saturates around 7–8 nm, meaning the relationship is not linear throughout but rather asymptotic toward the bulk value. This is not a contradiction but does limit the hypothesis to the ultra-thin regime (below ~8 nm). The 'medium' confidence tag on D8's Tc_K also introduces slight measurement uncertainty.

alternative_explanations: Thickness-dependent disorder: thinner NbSe2 films have higher sheet resistance (entering dirty limit), and increased electron-electron interactions or pair-breaking from surface/interface scattering suppress Tc independent of any intrinsic quantum size effect.

Surface oxidation or substrate interaction: very thin films may have a larger fraction of their volume affected by the Si substrate or surface oxide, chemically suppressing superconductivity rather than through a purely physical thickness effect.

Layer-number quantization in NbSe2 (a van der Waals material): Tc suppression at the ultra-thin limit may reflect discrete monolayer physics (e.g., loss of 3D phonon coupling, CDW competition) rather than a smooth continuum thickness dependence.
Proximity effect from the substrate: Si (high-resistivity) is not superconducting, but at sub-2 nm thicknesses, interface phonon coupling changes could shift Tc, mimicking a thickness effect.

The seeding literature citation (Chang_2025) concerns Ta with Au encapsulation, where a negative thickness-gap correlation arises from proximity-effect pair-breaking by the normal metal Au layer — a mechanistically different and potentially confounding conceptual frame for interpreting NbSe2 thickness effects.
missing_evidence: Samples below 1.99 nm (monolayer or bilayer NbSe2) to confirm the ultra-thin suppression trend continues and to test whether superconductivity is fully quenched at the monolayer limit.
Films thicker than 10 nm to confirm saturation at bulk Tc and rule out any non-monotonic behavior at larger thicknesses.
Direct sheet resistance or residual resistivity ratio (RRR) measurements paired with each thickness to decouple disorder effects from intrinsic quantum-size effects.
Samples on substrates other than Si (e.g., SiO2, hBN, sapphire) to assess substrate-interface contributions to Tc suppression.
Independent replication from groups other than Zaman_2025; all 6 data points come from a single study, limiting generalizability.
Structural characterization (XRD, TEM, SAED) confirming layer count and crystallinity as a function of thickness to distinguish disorder-driven from quantum-size-effect-driven Tc suppression.
Measurements at intermediate thicknesses (2–4 nm range with finer resolution) to better characterize the steepest part of the Tc-vs-thickness curve.
reasoning.confidence_rationale: The 6 samples all derive from a single study (Zaman_2025) on a single material (NbSe2) with a single substrate and deposition method, eliminating confounds from material or process heterogeneity. The monotonic trend is unambiguous and physically well-motivated (ultra-thin superconductor physics). However, confidence is capped below 0.7 because: (1) all evidence comes from one research group with no independent replication; (2) n=6 is small; (3) the saturation behavior in D5/D8 introduces some ambiguity about the functional form; (4) the mechanistic driver (disorder vs. quantum size effect vs. substrate proximity) cannot be distinguished from the available data alone. The prior approved finding [derived_BCS_gap_meV_vs_film_thickness_nm__nbse2] at confidence 0.55 is consistent and complementary, since BCS gap scales directly with Tc, providing mild corroborating support. The seeding literature (Chang_2025, Ta+Au) is mechanistically distinct and does not directly support or contradict the NbSe2 finding.
reasoning.derived_field_concern: The prior finding [derived_BCS_gap_meV_vs_film_thickness_nm__nbse2] was flagged as a derived_field_artifact because the BCS gap was computed from Tc via the BCS relation (Δ = 1.764 k_B Tc), making it mathematically redundant with Tc. That concern does not apply to the present hypothesis, which directly examines Tc_K vs. film_thickness_nm using primary measured Tc values. No derived field dependency issue exists in this analysis.
reasoning.negative_result_note: Not applicable; a positive pattern is observed.
